caveats: a: BLS evidence is broader than six-digit mattress manufacturing.
a: Already-automated cutting, quilting, and compression tasks are excluded from the remaining opportunity.
rho: AI-tool adoption does not establish labor substitution.
rho: Marketing productivity may support volume rather than reduce labor.
e: The provided count uses a machinery EBITDA margin that may materially misclassify mattress firms.
e: Industry sources describe increasing imports and US assembly-only models, complicating the manufacturer lens.
s5: No six-digit qualifying-transfer denominator was found.
s5: Succession-plan fragility is not a transaction probability.
q: Culp's filing documents channel and import pressure but is a fabric supplier rather than a mattress manufacturer.
q: Capture varies greatly between differentiated premium brands, regional wholesale, private label, and commodity boxed mattresses.
d5: The cited ISPA comparison is a short-period industry result rather than a five-year forecast.
d5: Import share affects domestic manufacturer demand even when US consumer quantity is stable.
d5: Market value and unit demand moved differently.
o: An accountable operator may be offshore or a large brand even when a physical mattress remains necessary.
o: Domestic operator demand can decline faster than US consumption because compressed products are easier to import.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Wholesale retailer and distributor programs, private-label supply, direct-to-consumer e-commerce, contract hospitality or institutional sales, and replacement purchases, generally priced per mattress, foundation, set, or program.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal units, shells, mattress-fabric or spring-component suppliers, furniture retailers without manufacturing, used or refurbished mattress sellers, and sleep accessories classified outside NAICS 337910.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying innerspring, box spring, hybrid, foam, latex, compressed, and other noninnerspring mattresses on stock, custom, branded, private-label, hospitality, healthcare, or institutional bases to external retailers, distributors, direct consumers, and commercial customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for broader furniture manufacturing, while NIST identifies manufacturing AI uses in inspection, maintenance, forecasting, and process decisions. I map high exposure to demand planning, marketing content, customer service, order entry, purchasing, scheduling, documentation, and warranty triage; partial exposure to product design, pattern nesting, quality, and supervision; and low exposure to cutting, sewing, spring or foam preparation, quilting, tape edging, compression, packing, handling, and maintenance execution. Broad furniture data include woodworking absent from most mattress plants and may misstate the relevant mix.
caveats: BLS evidence is broader than six-digit mattress manufacturing.
Already-automated cutting, quilting, and compression tasks are excluded from the remaining opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census across 10-12 independent mattress plants, including time studies for planning, customer service, design, cutting, quilting, assembly, inspection, compression, and logistics, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Commercial, planning, and quality-analysis work offers some substitution, but mattress manufacturing is already process-oriented and retains substantial hands-on cutting, sewing, assembly, and packing.

d5.bridge: ISPA reports total US mattress market value down 6.5% year over year and unit shipments down 13.2%, while Culp describes historically low domestic volume since fiscal 2022 and more recent signs of stabilization driven by replacement cycles. I use these as current market and supplier proxies, then estimate constant-price five-year demand for the current US mattress basket. A depressed base can make recovery stronger than the base estimate, while supplier commentary and one annual comparison may not represent the full market.
caveats: The cited ISPA comparison is a short-period industry result rather than a five-year forecast.
Import share affects domestic manufacturer demand even when US consumer quantity is stable.
Market value and unit demand moved differently.
d5.change_evidence: ISPA quarterly unit shipments by mattress type and origin, inflation-adjusted retail sell-through, housing formation, replacement-age surveys, hospitality procurement, and independent-manufacturer bookings would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Replacement purchases, population, household formation, hospitality, healthcare, and consumer sleep awareness provide a durable floor. Weak housing, affordability, long replacement intervals, post-pandemic pull-forward, imports, and consumer trade-down can keep real units subdued.

caveats: The provided count uses a machinery EBITDA margin that may materially misclassify mattress firms.
Industry sources describe increasing imports and US assembly-only models, complicating the manufacturer lens.
e.change_evidence: Resolve all 79 estimated in-band firms for ultimate ownership, actual domestic manufacturing, imported-content share, recurring external revenue, retailer concentration, brand or license rights, normalized EBITDA, and warranty liabilities.
e.rationale: Many in-band operators repeatedly manufacture for external retailers, brands, institutions, or direct customers and have transferable equipment, formulas, designs, brands, licenses, and channel relationships. Assembly-only import models, captive plants, retailer-owned units, distress, brand dependence, concentration, and errors from the machinery-margin bridge reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 337910 ($1,106,326,875) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 337910 ($9,004,566,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $380,325 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 337910); class <5: 92 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,298,435 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $254,753 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 61 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,324,541 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $259,875 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,621,125 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $514,265 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 79 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,971,519 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,152,612 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 37 firms, avg revenue/firm $55,736,351 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,935,472 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $480,872,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $94,347,234 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 79

caveats: An accountable operator may be offshore or a large brand even when a physical mattress remains necessary.
Domestic operator demand can decline faster than US consumption because compressed products are easier to import.
o.change_evidence: Consumer, retailer, hospitality, and healthcare interviews plus import, refurbishment, do-it-yourself, product-life, and alternative-sleep-product data would quantify self-service and elimination.
o.rationale: Customers still require accountable operators to design, source, cut, sew, assemble, test, label, compress, package, warrant, and deliver physical mattresses. Software can recommend products and facilitate sales but cannot provide the sleep surface; limited loss can come from imports, extended product life, refurbishment, customer assembly, and alternative sleep products.

caveats: Culp's filing documents channel and import pressure but is a fabric supplier rather than a mattress manufacturer.
Capture varies greatly between differentiated premium brands, regional wholesale, private label, and commodity boxed mattresses.
q.change_evidence: Wholesale, retail, license, and private-label contracts plus five-year SKU-level data for list-to-net price, promotional funding, rebates, returns, warranty, freight, commissions, and gross margin would reveal pass-through.
q.rationale: Brand, proprietary materials or construction, comfort positioning, retailer slots, delivery, and warranty can preserve some savings. Promotion-heavy retail, online comparison, private-label bids, warehouse clubs, powerful retailers, imports, returns, and frequent discounting share a large portion over five years.

rho.bridge: NIST reports 46% manufacturing AI-tool use and more than 80% expecting increased use within two years, while listing data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy integration barriers. I translate broad adoption into five-year implementation of exposed tasks. General manufacturing may overstate small bedding plants' data infrastructure, while e-commerce, customer-service, content, forecasting, and vision tools are readily accessible.
caveats: AI-tool adoption does not establish labor substitution.
Marketing productivity may support volume rather than reduce labor.
rho.change_evidence: Comparable-manufacturer deployment records showing baseline hours, forecast error, warranty resolution, order errors, inspection performance, integration cost, adoption, and sustained savings would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Demand, promotion, order, warranty, schedule, and visual-inspection tools can deploy, but SKU proliferation, retailer data separation, subjective comfort and appearance, variable soft materials, legacy equipment, and limited IT staff prevent full realization.

s5.bridge: Deloitte surveyed 500 US family-enterprise members and found only 24% of current-generation respondents strongly agreed their succession plan could withstand departure of an important family employee. I use this as broad succession-pressure evidence, then reduce potential exits for internal transfers, non-control investments, closures, bankruptcies without transferable operations, and failed sales. The survey is not bedding-specific, while current industry consolidation and distress can increase both deal flow and nonqualifying failures.
caveats: No six-digit qualifying-transfer denominator was found.
Succession-plan fragility is not a transaction probability.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and exit-intention outreach to resolved firms plus five years of closed control deals, bankruptcies, and plant closures from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, ISPA members, and bedding-industry advisers would narrow the range.
s5.rationale: Independent regional brands and contract manufacturers can attract strategic buyers or face succession, but weak demand, retailer concentration, brand risk, and imports may turn potential transfers into closures or distressed asset sales.

principal_driver: Recurring replacement of an essential physical sleep product supports long-run demand.
principal_weakness: Weak current units, imports, and promotion-heavy retail severely constrain domestic growth and value capture.

sources.supports: Defines innerspring, box spring, and noninnerspring mattresses made on stock or custom and assembled or knockdown bases.

sources.supports: Provides occupation-level employment and wages for the broader furniture industries, including sewing, upholstery, finishing, machine operation, and other production work.

sources.supports: Reports 46% manufacturing AI-tool use and more than 80% expecting increased use in two years, with operational use cases and adoption barriers.
sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports US mattress market value down 6.5% year over year and unit shipments down 13.2%, documenting current consumer-demand softness.
sources.title: ISPA 2026 Mattress Industry Trends Report overview

sources.supports: Describes historically low domestic mattress volume, replacement demand, consumer and housing pressure, imports, assembly-only models, e-commerce and warehouse-club channels, and compressed mattresses.
sources.title: Culp 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Describes a January 2024 survey of 500 US family-enterprise respondents and reports that 24% of current-generation respondents strongly agreed their succession plan would withstand departure of an important family employee.
sources.title: Deloitte Private survey: generational disparities in family-business succession planning

title: Mattress Manufacturing
